meta.title: Problem-Solving Skills Assessment
meta.description: A self-reflection test that explores how you tend to approach real-world problems across 4 dimensions of problem-solving: framing, creative thinking, decision-making, and execution.

levels.label: Master Problem-Solver
levels.desc: You consistently frame problems accurately, generate genuinely creative options, decide with clear judgment, and follow through to real results. Problem-solving is a core strength for you.

levels.label: Sharp Problem-Solver
levels.desc: You have strong instincts across framing, creativity, decisions, and execution. A few specific situations still trip you up, but you consistently get things done well.

levels.label: Capable Problem-Solver
levels.desc: You generally solve problems effectively, but pressure, habit, or impatience sometimes lead you to skip a step, like framing carefully or following through fully.

levels.label: Developing Problem-Solver
levels.desc: Your problem-solving is inconsistent — strong in some dimensions, reactive in others. Deliberate practice in a few key areas could make a real difference.

levels.label: Inconsistent Problem-Solver
levels.desc: You often jump to quick fixes, default answers, or give up before finishing. Small, deliberate changes to how you approach problems could help a lot.

levels.label: Needs Practice
levels.desc: Your answers suggest problem-solving habits like framing, creativity, deciding, and following through rarely guide your approach right now. This is a good starting point for focused practice.

questions.text: You're handed a vague complaint like 'sales are down' with no other details. How likely are you to dig into the specifics before proposing a fix?
A colleague describes a 'broken' process, but their explanation focuses only on the most recent incident. How likely are you to ask what happened before that incident to see the fuller pattern?
You're assigned a project with a fuzzy goal. How likely are you to ask clarifying questions before diving into the work?
Your team is debating how to fix a recurring issue, but no one has asked why it keeps happening. How likely are you to steer the conversation toward the root cause first?
Two teammates describe the same problem in completely different ways. How likely are you to reconcile their perspectives before deciding how to proceed?
An urgent issue lands on your desk and everyone wants an immediate fix. How likely are you to resist grabbing the first fix and take a moment to understand what's actually wrong?
A plan you're reviewing rests on an assumption that seems shaky. How likely are you to question that assumption rather than proceed as if it's solid?
You're under deadline pressure and the problem statement is unclear. How likely are you to still take a few minutes to clarify it rather than guess and go?
A new problem looks a lot like one you solved before. How likely are you to check for meaningful differences instead of assuming the old solution will work?
A client insists on a specific fix, but you suspect it won't address their actual need. How likely are you to probe further to understand what they're really trying to achieve?
You're about to start solving a problem someone else described to you. How likely are you to restate it in your own words to check you understood correctly?
A stakeholder tells you to 'just fix X.' How likely are you to ask what outcome they're actually hoping for before acting?
You're getting conflicting reports about what caused an issue. How likely are you to investigate further before settling on an explanation?
You could jump straight into implementing the first fix that comes to mind without diagnosing the actual problem. How likely are you to do this?
You're facing a large, messy problem with no clear starting point. How likely are you to break it into smaller, well-defined pieces before tackling it?
You could assume you already understand a new problem because it looks similar to something you've seen before, and skip investigating it properly. How likely are you to do this?
A problem is affecting people you don't normally interact with. How likely are you to talk to them directly before deciding how to frame the issue?
You're told there's no time for questions, just fix it. How likely are you to still jot down what's actually being asked before starting?
A proposed 'solution' is being discussed, but it doesn't seem to actually address the original problem. How likely are you to point that out?
You could get impatient with someone's clarifying questions and push the group straight to action. How likely are you to do this?
An issue keeps recurring despite repeated fixes. How likely are you to look for the underlying cause instead of patching the symptom again?
A team disagreement is turning into 'whose fault is this' instead of 'what's actually broken.' How likely are you to redirect the conversation toward diagnosing the real issue?
There are several plausible explanations for why something went wrong. How likely are you to consider more than one before settling on a cause?
You could accept the first explanation offered for a problem without checking whether it actually accounts for everything that happened. How likely are you to do this?
You're about to start work on a request. How likely are you to first ask what success will actually look like when it's done?
You've hit a wall with a familiar approach that isn't working. How likely are you to deliberately try something unconventional instead of repeating the same method harder?
You're brainstorming solutions to a problem. How likely are you to generate a wide range of ideas, even unlikely ones, before narrowing down?
A problem you're facing resembles a challenge from a completely different field. How likely are you to borrow an idea or analogy from that field?
Your first idea for solving a problem seems to work okay. How likely are you to still push yourself to think of alternatives that might work better?
Someone suggests an unconventional idea in a meeting, and you could dismiss it quickly because 'that's not how we do things.' How likely are you to do this?
You notice two unrelated ideas that might combine into something new. How likely are you to explore combining them rather than treating them separately?
A teammate shares a wild, half-formed idea during a brainstorm. How likely are you to build on it encouragingly rather than shut it down early?
You're stuck on a problem. How likely are you to deliberately try viewing it from someone else's perspective, like a customer or a total outsider?
You could just go with the first workable solution instead of exploring whether a better one exists. How likely are you to do this?
You have a rough idea for a solution. How likely are you to quickly sketch or test a prototype rather than overthink it in your head first?
You're trying to open up new possibilities for a stuck problem. How likely are you to ask a series of 'what if' questions to explore it?
You notice a pattern that connects two problems that seemed unrelated. How likely are you to explore what that connection might mean?
A tried-and-tested method isn't working as well as it used to, but you could keep using it anyway instead of brainstorming alternatives. How likely are you to do this?
You're looking for inspiration to solve a problem. How likely are you to look outside your usual field, like art, nature, or another industry, for ideas?
A process exists mainly because 'we've always done it this way.' How likely are you to challenge that assumption to look for a better approach?
A plan you tried has failed. How likely are you to quickly generate several alternative approaches rather than getting stuck?
Someone gives you unconventional feedback that challenges an idea you're attached to. How likely are you to genuinely consider it rather than get defensive?
You could hold back a genuinely novel idea in a meeting because you're worried it sounds strange, staying quiet instead of sharing it. How likely are you to do this?
You've found a solution that technically works but feels clunky. How likely are you to keep looking for a simpler, more elegant one?
You're trying to explain or explore a tricky problem. How likely are you to use an analogy or metaphor to think it through?
Your days are usually full of reacting to urgent issues. How likely are you to deliberately set aside time to think creatively about bigger problems?
You could avoid experimenting with a new idea because it might fail publicly, even though it could work. How likely are you to hold back because of that fear?
A group discussion is generating ideas, and someone else's idea is close but not quite right. How likely are you to build on it rather than only pushing your own idea?
You're stuck on a problem and want a fresh angle. How likely are you to try imagining the complete opposite of your usual approach to spark new ideas?
A wildly different solution has come up that would mean starting over. How likely are you to seriously consider it rather than dismiss it because of the effort already spent?
You're facing a decision with real consequences either way. How likely are you to carefully weigh the pros and cons before committing?
You need to decide but don't have complete information, and waiting longer has real costs. How likely are you to make a timely call anyway rather than delay indefinitely?
An option looks appealing mainly because of its upside. How likely are you to also seriously consider its downside and risks before choosing it?
A decision could drag on for weeks because you're afraid of choosing wrong. How likely are you to let it drag on instead of deciding?
A decision you're about to make will affect other people. How likely are you to ask for their input before finalizing it?
A decision you made is turning out to be wrong. How likely are you to change course quickly rather than keep defending it?
One option is more convenient right now, but another is better in the long run. How likely are you to weigh the long-term consequences seriously before choosing?
You could default to the easiest option even though you suspect a better one exists, just to avoid the extra effort. How likely are you to do this?
You're comparing several options for an important decision. How likely are you to use clear criteria to compare them rather than just going with a gut feeling?
Two good options are on the table and a decision needs to be made. How likely are you to actually choose rather than get stuck endlessly weighing them?
You could let strong emotions in the moment override careful reasoning on an important decision. How likely are you to do this?
You're about to finalize a high-stakes decision. How likely are you to seek a second opinion before committing?
You're weighing a decision that can't easily be undone once made. How likely are you to be more careful and deliberate with it than with a decision you could easily reverse?
Someone else made a similar decision recently, and you could just copy what they did without checking whether it actually fits your situation. How likely are you to do this?
You keep putting off a decision without making progress. How likely are you to set yourself a deadline to force a choice?
You're deciding something that feels urgent right now. How likely are you to also consider how it will look a year from now before committing?
A necessary decision would be unpopular with some people. How likely are you to avoid making it just to sidestep the pushback?
You're facing a decision but realize there's important information you don't have yet. How likely are you to go find that information rather than decide blind?
You have several competing priorities demanding a decision. How likely are you to decide based on actual impact rather than just whoever is asking loudest?
You could base a decision on who's pushing hardest for it rather than what's actually the best choice. How likely are you to do this?
An option carries some risk, but the potential upside clearly outweighs it. How likely are you to accept that calculated risk rather than always play it safe?
New information comes in that changes the picture after you've already started down a path. How likely are you to update your decision rather than stick with the old plan out of stubbornness?
A decision you're making involves real trade-offs that affect others. How likely are you to explain your reasoning transparently to the people affected?
A decision is difficult, and you could put it off hoping the problem resolves itself on its own. How likely are you to do this instead of addressing it?
You've made a considered decision. How likely are you to commit to it fully rather than constantly second-guess yourself afterward?
You've settled on a plan to solve a problem. How likely are you to follow through on it fully rather than lose steam partway through?
You have a big task ahead with no clear starting point. How likely are you to break it into concrete next steps and actually start on them?
You're partway through executing a plan. How likely are you to track your progress and adjust when something isn't working?
You could start a plan enthusiastically and then abandon it as soon as something more interesting comes along. How likely are you to do this?
You're about to start a project with several likely obstacles. How likely are you to anticipate those obstacles and prepare for them ahead of time?
You've assigned a task to someone, or handed off part of a plan. How likely are you to follow up to confirm it was actually completed?
You could agree to a deadline you already suspect you can't realistically meet, just to avoid an uncomfortable conversation. How likely are you to do this?
Circumstances change partway through a project. How likely are you to adjust the plan without losing sight of the original goal?
A task you started has become tedious. How likely are you to hold yourself accountable and finish it anyway?
You could wait for perfect conditions before starting a task instead of taking the first practical step now. How likely are you to keep waiting?
A task you're working on is running into real difficulty. How likely are you to communicate that proactively rather than go quiet until it's finished?
You have a solution ready to roll out. How likely are you to test it on a small scale first rather than deploy it everywhere at once?
You're under time pressure to finish something. How likely are you to cut corners even though it compromises the quality of the result?
A project you led didn't go entirely as planned. How likely are you to review what went wrong afterward rather than just move on?
You're partway through a long project and the initial excitement has faded. How likely are you to keep the momentum going anyway?
A task turns out to be more than you can realistically finish alone. How likely are you to delegate or ask for help rather than struggle through it solo?
A plan is clearly not working, but you could keep pushing forward with it anyway instead of adapting. How likely are you to do this?
You're planning a project with a few parts that are most likely to go wrong. How likely are you to prepare a backup plan for those specific parts?
A task has both an exciting part and a tedious part. How likely are you to actually finish the tedious part rather than only do the fun bits?
Once the planning and ideas phase of a project is done, you could procrastinate on the actual execution because the 'fun part' feels over. How likely are you to do this?
You're managing a task with many moving pieces. How likely are you to use checklists, schedules, or similar tools to keep it on track?
A solution has been rolled out and real-world results are coming in. How likely are you to revisit and refine it based on that feedback?
You could rush execution just to be able to say something is 'done,' even though you know it isn't really solved well. How likely are you to do this?
You made a mistake partway through executing a plan. How likely are you to take ownership of it and fix it rather than hide it?
No one is checking up on a commitment you made. How likely are you to follow through on it anyway?
